draft.introduction: Longchenpa (1308–1364) is the supreme systematizer of Dzogchen, the “Great Perfection” teaching of Tibetan Buddhism. His works describe awareness (rigpa) as intrinsically pure, needing no modification.

translation.text: The nature of mind is primordially pure, luminous, and uncompounded. It is not produced by causes, not destroyed by conditions. It is like the sky—without center or periphery. All phenomena arise within it like clouds in the sky, without altering its nature.
translation.original_text: sems nyid ye nas dag pa ’od gsal ’dus ma byas

translation.text: Do not seek enlightenment elsewhere. Do not reject samsara. Rest in the natural state of awareness, which has never been stained by confusion. This resting is not an act of will but the relaxation of all acts of will.
translation.original_text: byang chub gzhan du ma tshol | ’khor ba ma spang | rig pa’i rang bzhag la zhog

translator_notes.note: Dzogchen’s claim that awareness is “primordially pure” (ka-dag) distinguishes it from gradual-path Buddhism, which sees purity as the result of practice. In Dzogchen, practice does not create purity but recognizes it. The difference is between construction and discovery.
